elements.sent-2411-001: Reinforcement Learning (RL) mathematically formulates decision-making with Markov Decision Process (MDP).
elements.sent-2411-002: With MDPs, researchers have achieved remarkable breakthroughs across various domains, including games, robotics, and language models.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper seeks a new possibility, Natural Language Reinforcement Learning (NLRL), by extending traditional MDP to natural language-based representation space.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, NLRL innovatively redefines RL principles, including task objectives, policy, value function, Bellman equation, and policy iteration, into their language counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-005: With recent advancements in large language models (LLMs), NLRL can be practically implemented to achieve RL-like policy and value improvement by either pure prompting or gradient-based training.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments over Maze, Breakthrough, and Tic-Tac-Toe games demonstrate the effectiveness, efficiency, and interpretability of the NLRL framework among diverse use cases.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our code will be released at \url{https://github.com/waterhorse1/Natural-language-RL .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Reinforcement Learning (RL) \citep{sutton2018reinforcement provides a rigorous framework -- Markov Decision Process (MDP) for solving general decision-making problems.}
elements.sent-2411-009: It transforms the policy learning problem into a mathematical optimization task.
elements.sent-2411-010: While RL has achieved breakthroughs across various domains, several challenges remain.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, traditional RL algorithms generally lack task-specific prior knowledge, requiring extensive sampling to approximate environment dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-013: Even in superhuman-performing models like AlphaZero \citep{silver2017alphazero , strategic reasoning remains elusive, even to professional players.}
elements.sent-2411-014: RL training is also unstable \citep{zheng2023secrets, andrychowicz2020matters due to its reliance on scalar rewards as the sole supervision signal.}
elements.sent-2411-015: This one-dimensional feedback is particularly limiting in real-world scenarios where richer, multi-modal signals are naturally available, such as textual feedback~\citep{bai2022constitutional,madaan2024self , visual demonstrations~\citep{bousmalis2023robocat,xu2024sinvig , or other sensory inputs~\citep{qiao2024autoact .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: To tackle these challenges, we seek a new RL paradigm shift, inspired by language-centric decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-017: Unlike traditional RL, which relies heavily on formalized mathematical modeling, humans can leverage natural language to interpret tasks, devise strategies, and communicate their reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-018: This language-driven approach enables rapid generalization using text-based prior knowledge, enhances interpretability through explicit reasoning, and provides access to rich, informative signals from linguistic data.
elements.sent-2411-019: Thus, natural language represents a largely untapped resource for improving the efficiency, stability, and interoperability of RL systems.
elements.sent-2411-020: The recent success of language-based transformers \citep{vaswani2017attention further opens new avenues for integrating language into the RL framework.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their unprecedented proficiency in generating, understanding, and processing complex language-based information.
elements.sent-2411-022: Building upon language-centric decision-making and advancement of LLMs, we introduce Natural Language Reinforcement Learning (NLRL), a novel RL paradigm that combines RL's mathematical rigor with the representational richness of natural language.
elements.sent-2411-023: In NLRL, core RL components—such as task objectives, policies, value functions, and the Bellman equation—are reinterpreted as language-based constructs.
elements.sent-2411-024: The medium of natural language largely facilitates the integration of prior knowledge stored in LLMs, and effectively translates decision-making processes into a form that is both intuitive and interpretable.
elements.sent-2411-025: NLRL also provides a systematic solution for leveraging rich textual feedback in sequential decision-making tasks, enabling stable training.
elements.sent-2411-026: Building on this new paradigm, we can efficiently implement RL algorithms in language representation space, resulting in various LLM applications.
elements.sent-2411-027: Leveraging the unsupervised nature of RL, all training and improvements can be achieved through environment interaction without the need for labeled data from humans or advanced models.
elements.sent-2411-028: In particular, Sec~\ref{sec:use_case_1 illustrates how NLRL algorithms can enhance an LLM agent’s critique and planning abilities using pure prompting.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Sec~\ref{sec:use_case_2 details the training of a natural language value function to serve as an evaluator or validator, providing reliable assessments for any given state.}
elements.sent-2411-030: A comprehensive NLRL training pipeline, covering iterative training of both language policies and critics, is outlined in Sec~\ref{sec:use_case_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Similar to the traditional actor-critic framework \citep{barto1983neuronlike, sutton1999policy , this pipeline enables the LLM to learn purely from textual environmental feedback, generating language-based Chain-of-Thought \citep{wei2022cot0 enhanced policies and critiques.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We empirically validate these use cases in environments such as the Maze game, Breakthrough board game, and Tic-Tac-Toe, demonstrating the effectiveness and superiority of the NLRL framework.
elements.sent-2411-033: Reinforcement Learning models the decision-making problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), defined by the state space $\mathcal{S}$, action space $\mathcal{A}$, probabilistic transition function $P: \mathcal{S \times \mathcal{A \times \mathcal{S \rightarrow[0,1]$, discount factor $\gamma \in[0,1)$ and reward function $r: \mathcal{S \times \mathcal{A \rightarrow\left[-R_{\max , R_{\max }\right]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The goal of RL aims to learn a policy $\pi: \mathcal{S \times \mathcal{A \rightarrow[0,1]$, which measures the action $a$'s probability given the state $s$: $\pi(a|s)=\operatorname{Pr}\left(A_t=a \mid S_t=s\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: In decision-making tasks, the optimal policy tends to maximize the expected discounted cumulative reward: $\pi^{* (a|s)=\arg\max_{\pi}\mathbb{E _{\pi}\big[\sum_{t=0 ^{\infty \gamma^t r\left(\mathrm{s _t, \mathrm{a _t\right)\big]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The state-action and state value functions are two key concepts that evaluate states or state-action pairs by measuring the cumulative reward starting from them: $Q_\pi(s_t,a_t)=\mathbb{E _{\mathrm{(s,a) _{t+1: \infty \sim P_\pi}\left[ \sum_{i=t ^\infty \gamma^{i-t r\left(s_i,a_i\right) \mid s_t, a_t\right]$, $V_\pi(s_{t ) = \mathbb{E _{a_{t , \mathrm{(s,a) _{t+1: \infty \sim P_\pi}\left[\sum_{i=t ^\infty \gamma^{i-t r\left(s_i,a_i\right) \mid s_t\right]$, where $P_{\pi}$ is the trajectory distribution given the policy $\pi$ and dynamic transition $P$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Given the definition of $V_\pi(s_{t )$, the relationship between temporally adjacent state's value (e.g., $V(s_{t )$ and $V(s_{t+1 )$) can be derived as the Bellman expectation equation \citep{bellman1965dynamic .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Here is an example of one-step Bellman expectation equation: \begin{equation}\label{equ:one-step-bellman} V_\pi(s_t)=\mathbb{E}_{a_t\sim\pi_\theta}\Big[r(s_t,a_t)+\gamma \mathbb{E}_{s_{t+1}\sim p(s_{t+1}|s_t,a_t)}[V_\pi(s_{t+1})]\Big], \forall s_{t} \in \mathcal{S}\end{equation} A similar equation can also be derived for $Q_\pi(s,a)$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Given these basic RL definitions and equations, many RL algorithms fall in the scope of generalized policy iteration (GPI).
elements.sent-2411-040: GPI is an iterative process and each iteration consists of two procedures: policy evaluation and policy improvement.. Policy Evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-041: The target of the policy evaluation process is to estimate state value function $V_\pi(s)$ or state-action value function $Q_\pi(s, a)$ for a given policy $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-042: For simplicity, we only utilize $V_\pi(s)$ in the following illustration.
elements.sent-2411-043: Two common value function estimation methods are the Monte Carlo (MC) estimate and the Temporal-Difference (TD) estimate \citep{sutton1988learning .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Starting from the definition of $V_\pi(s_t)$, the Monte Carlo (MC) estimate uses sampling over complete trajectories to calculate an unbiased estimate: $V_\pi(s_t) \approx \frac{1 {K \sum_{n=1 ^{K \left[\sum_{i=t ^\infty \gamma^{i-t r(s_i^n, a_i^n)\right]$ where we average the cumulative rewards over multiple full paths starting from $s_t$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The Temporal Difference (TD) estimate, on the other hand, builds on the temporal relationship between states without requiring complete trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-046: It estimates $V_\pi(s_t)$ using the immediate reward and the estimated value of the next state: $V_\pi(s_t) \approx \frac{1 {K \sum_{n=1 ^{K \left[r(s_t, a_t^n) + \gamma V_\pi(s_{t+1 ^n)\right]$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: This approach effectively uses a "bootstrap" by depending on the estimated value of the next state to approximate $V_\pi(s_t)$.. Policy Improvement.
elements.sent-2411-048: The policy improvement process aims to update and improve policy according to the result from the policy evaluation process.
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, it replaces the old policy $\pi_{\text{old }$ with the new one $\pi_{\text{new }$ to increase the expected return: $V_{\pi_{\text {new (s_0) \geq V_{\pi_{\text {old (s_0)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In the environment with small, discrete action spaces, such improvements can be achieved by greedily choosing the action that maximizes $Q_{\pi_{\text {old (s, a)$ at each state: \begin{equation}\pi_{\text {new}}(\cdot \mid s)=\underset{\bar{\pi}(\cdot \mid s) \in \mathcal{P}(\mathcal{A})}{\arg \max } \mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{a} \sim \bar{\pi}}\left[Q_{\pi_{\text {old}}}(s, \mathrm{a})\right], \forall s \in \mathcal{S},\end{equation} Another improvement method involves applying policy gradient ascent \citep{sutton1999policy .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\pi_{\text {new}}(\cdot \mid s)=\underset{\bar{\pi}(\cdot \mid s) \in P(A)}{\arg \max } E_{a \sim \bar{\pi}}\left[Q_{\pi_{\text {old}}}(s, a)\right], \forall s \in S,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-051: It parameterizes the policy $\pi_{\theta}$ with $\theta$ and we can derive the analytical policy gradient: \begin{equation}\left.\nabla_\theta V_{\pi_\theta}\left(s_{0}\right)\right|_{\theta=\theta_{\text {old }}} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{(s,a)} \sim P_{\pi_{\theta_{\text {old }}}}}\left[\left.\nabla_\theta\log{\pi_{\theta}(a|s)} Q_{\pi_{\theta_{\text {old }}}}(\mathrm{s}, \mathrm{a})\right|_{\theta=\theta_{\text {old }}}\right],\end{equation} Where $s_{0}$ denotes the initial state.
elements.sent-2411-052: By choosing a small step-size $\alpha > 0$ to conduct gradient ascent: $\theta_{\text {new =\theta+\left.\alpha \nabla_\theta V_{\pi_{\theta (s_0)\right|_{\theta=\theta_{\text {old }$, we can guarantee the policy improvement: $V_{\pi_{\text {new (s_0) \geq V_{\pi_{\text {old (s_0)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: In contrast to the precise statistical models used in traditional RL, NLRL frames all elements—including task objectives, value evaluations, and strategic policies—within the form of natural language.
elements.sent-2411-054: This section aims to navigate decision-making tasks using natural language, aligning it with traditional RL concepts, definitions, and equations.
elements.sent-2411-055: Due to the inherent ambiguity of natural language, the equations presented here are not strictly derived from mathematical definitions.
elements.sent-2411-056: Instead, they are analogical and based on empirical insights into original RL concepts.
elements.sent-2411-057: We leave rigorous theoretical definition and analysis for future work.
elements.sent-2411-058: We start with analogies in traditional RL to model NLRL.
elements.sent-2411-059: We provide Fig. \ref{fig:main_fig to illustrate most concepts discussed in this section.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Language Task instruction: For decision-making tasks, NLRL defines a natural language task instruction $T_{L}$, like ``reaching the goal" or ``opening the door".
elements.sent-2411-061: Then, we denote a metric by $F$ that measures the completeness of the task instruction given the trajectory description $D_{L (\tau_{\pi )$, where $D_{L}$ is a language descriptor that can transform the trajectory distribution $\tau_{\pi}$ into its corresponding language description $D_L(\tau_{\pi )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The objective of NLRL is reformulated as \begin{equation}\max_{\pi} F(D_L(\tau_{\pi}), T_{L})\end{equation} That is, NLRL is trying to optimize the policy so that the language description of the trajectory distribution $\tau_{\pi}$ can show high completeness of the task instruction.
elements.sent-2411-063: Language Policy: Instead of directly modeling action probability, NLRL determines the action with chain-of-thought process \citep{wei2022cot0 , including strategic thoughts, logical reasoning, and planning.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Thus, we represent the policy on language as $\pi_{L (a, c|s) = \pi_{L (c|s)\pi_{L (a|c,s)$, which will first generate such thought process $\pi_{L (c|s)$, then output the final action probability $\pi(a|c,s)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Language Value Function: Similar to the definition of $Q$ and $V$ in traditional RL, NLRL leverages language value function, relying on natural language evaluation to assess the policy effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-066: The language state value function $V^{L _\pi$ and language state-action value function $Q^{L _\pi$ are defined as: \begin{equation}\label{equ:lvf} Q^{L}_\pi(s_t,a_t)=D\left(\mathrm{\left(s,a\right)}_{t+1: \infty}\sim P_\pi \mid s_t, a_t, T_{L}\right), V^{L}_\pi(s_{t}) = D\left(\mathrm{a_{t}, \left(s,a\right)}_{t+1: \infty}\sim P_\pi \mid s_t, T_{L}\right)\end{equation} Given the current state $s_t$ or state-action $(s_t,a_t)$, $Q^{L _\pi$ and $V^{L _\pi$ leverage language descriptions instead of scalar value to demonstrate the effectiveness of policy for achieving the task objective $T_{L}$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{equ:lvf} Q^{L}_\pi(s_t,a_t)=D\left(\left(s,a\right)_{t+1: \infty}\sim P_\pi \mid s_t, a_t, T_{L}\right), V^{L}_\pi(s_{t}) = D\left(\mathrm{a_{t}, \left(s,a\right)}_{t+1: \infty}\sim P_\pi \mid s_t, T_{L}\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-067: The language value functions are intuitively rich in the information of values and enhance interpretability rather than the traditional scalar-based value.
elements.sent-2411-068: It can represent the evaluation results from different perspectives, consisting of the underlying logic/thoughts, prediction/analysis of future outcomes, comparison among different actions, etc. Language Bellman Equation: In the traditional Bellman equation (Equ.~\ref{equ:one-step-bellman ), the state evaluation value $V_\pi(s_t)$, can be decomposed into two parts: : (1) the intermediate transition, which include immediate $a_{t}$, reward $r_{t}$, and next state $s_{t+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Based on such decomposition intuition, we argue that the language value function $V^{L _\pi$ should analogically satisfy the language Bellman equation Equ.\ref{equ:langueg_bellman_exp . \begin{equation}\label{equ:langueg_bellman_exp} V_\pi^{L}(s_t)=G_1^{a_{t},s_{t+1}\sim P_\pi}\Big(G_2\left(d\left(a_t,r\left(s_t,a_t\right),s_{t+1}),V_\pi^L(s_{t+1}\right)\right) \Big), \forall s_{t} \in \mathcal{S},\end{equation} where $d\left(a_t,r\left(s_t,a_t\right),s_{t+1 )\right)$ depicts the language description of intermediate transition, while $G_{1}$ and $G_{2}$ serves as two information aggregation functions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: By drawing an analogy to Equ.~\ref{equ:one-step-bellman , $G_{2}$ mimics the add the summation operation `$+$' in the original Bellman equation, aggregating information from intermediate transition's description $d$ and future evaluation $V_\pi^L(s_{t+1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Meanwhile, $G_1$ serves the role of the expectation operator $\mathbb{E}$, aggregating information accross different $(a_{t ,s_{t+1 )$ pairs by sampling from the transition distribution $P_{\pi}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: In this part, we introduce how language GPI is conducted.
elements.sent-2411-073: Similar to traditional GPI, language GPI also consists of two procedures--language policy evaluation and language policy improvement.
elements.sent-2411-074: Language policy evaluation aims to estimate language value function $V^{L _\pi$ and $Q^{L _\pi$ for each state.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: We present how two classical estimations: MC and TD estimate work in language policy evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-076: Starting from the state $s_{t}$, MC estimate is conducted over text rollouts (i.e. $K$ full trajectories $\{a_{t , \left(s,a\right)_{t+1: \infty}\}$) given the policy $\pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Since we cannot take the average operation in language space, we instead leverage language aggregator $G_{1}$ to aggregate information over finite trajectories, approximating the expected evaluation: \begin{equation}\label{equ:mc_estimate} V^{L}_\pi(s_{t})\approx G_{1}\left(\left\{\mathrm{a_{t}^n, \left(s,a\right)}_{t+1: \infty}^{n}\right\}_{n=1}^{K}\right),\end{equation} \begin{wrapfigure {r {0.44\textwidth \includegraphics[width=0.44\textwidth] {figs/td.pdf \end{wrapfigure Language Temporal-Difference Estimate.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Language TD estimate mainly relies on the one-step language Bellman equation illustrated in Equ.. \ref{equ:langueg_bellman_exp .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Similar to the language MC estimate, we aggregate $K$ one-step samples to approximate the expected evaluation: \begin{equation}\label{eqa:td_estimate} V_\pi^{L}(s_t)\approx G_1\Big(\big\{G_2\big(d(s_t,a_t^n,r(s_t,a_t^n),s_{t+1}^n),V_\pi^L(s_{t+1}^n)\big)\big\}_{n=1}^K \Big), \forall s_{t} \in \mathcal{S},\end{equation} where $d$, $G_1$ and $G_2$ has the same meaning for that in Equ.\ref{equ:langueg_bellman_exp .}

elements.sent-2411-089: Section \ref{sec:nlrl outlines the core philosophy of NLRL, which involves translating key concepts from RL into their human natural language counterparts.}
elements.sent-2411-090: To practically implement these key concepts, a model capable of understanding, processing, and generating language is essential.
elements.sent-2411-091: Large language models (LLMs), trained on vast corpora of human language and knowledge, emerge as a natural choice to emulate human behaviour and implement language-based RL components.
elements.sent-2411-092: In the following sections, we demonstrate how LLMs can function in various roles within the NLRL framework.
elements.sent-2411-093: Many works adopted LLMs as the decision-making agent \citep{wang2023voyager, feng2023chessgpt, christianos2023pangu, yao2022react with Chain-of-thought process \citep{wei2022cot0 .}}
elements.sent-2411-094: By setting proper instructions, LLMs can leverage natural language to describe their underlying thought for determining the action, akin to human strategic thinking.
elements.sent-2411-095: The original idea of value function approximation \citep{sutton1999policy is to use a parameterized one-dimensional output function to replace the big value table and serve as a value function approximator.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Such design largely helps RL to handle high-dimensional and large-scale decision-making problems.

elements.sent-2411-148: Specifically, we aim to train a language value function with language TD in the 5x5 breakthrough board game (Fig~\ref{Fig:5x5_breakthrough ).}
elements.sent-2411-149: Breakthrough is a turn-based board game for two players, white and black.
elements.sent-2411-150: Each player has a few lines of pawns.
elements.sent-2411-151: Black moves first and in each turn, the player chooses one piece to move one square forward (straight or diagonal) onto empty squares or capture diagonally.
elements.sent-2411-152: The goal is to break through the opponent's line and reach their home row.
elements.sent-2411-153: As mentioned in Sec~\ref{sec:use_case_2 , this value function can serve as a reliable board evaluator/annotator.}
elements.sent-2411-154: However, evaluating board states in this game is challenging for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-155: First, the 5x5 breakthrough variant is niche, leading to sparse pre-training data on board evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-156: Most LLMs have minimal knowledge of it.
elements.sent-2411-157: Second, despite the small board size, its state space complexity can reach $10^8$ \citep{saffidine2012solving , making natural language assessments significantly more difficult.}

elements.sent-2411-163: In our experiment, our $\pi$ is the Monte-Carlo Tree-Search (MCTS) algorithm \citep{kocsis2006mcts-bandit from OpenSpiel \citep{LanctotEtAl2019OpenSpiel , with a high number of simulations and rollouts to ensure an extensive search.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: Since $V_\pi^L(s)$ is over state $s$, our second step is to build a state dataset $D_s$--the distribution $P(s)$ that our $V_\pi^L$ works on.
elements.sent-2411-166: To ensure that $V_\pi^L$ can assess positions across different levels of gameplay, we build a mixed state dataset.
elements.sent-2411-167: Specifically, we maintain a pool of MCTs policies, where each policy variant is characterized by a distinct number of simulations and rollouts.
elements.sent-2411-168: The mixed state dataset is then created by pairwise combining policies from this pool, running rollouts for each policy pair, and merging the resulting data from all combinations.

elements.sent-2411-176: The final step is to develop an automatic metric for evaluating the language value function/board evaluation, which is challenging even for advanced LLMs like GPT-4o, given their limited domain knowledge.

elements.sent-2411-193: Despite the soundness of Sec~\ref{sec:use_case_3 , we identify training instability resulting from the language value function, mainly from two perspectives.}
elements.sent-2411-194: First, we observed that the language value function $V_\pi^L$ can easily hallucinate when evaluating unseen state-action pairs, leading to incorrect language policy improvement.
elements.sent-2411-195: To address this, we add an action selection mask to restrict language policy improvement only to moves that are likely to be chosen by the language policy $\pi_L$ during rollout.
elements.sent-2411-196: Instead of prompting LLM to select from all available moves, we sample the language policy $N_{sample}$ times and build an action candidate list.

elements.sent-2411-199: Second, we encountered the challenge of catastrophic forgetting for value training.
elements.sent-2411-200: Specifically, LLM can rapidly overfit to the state-action pairs encountered in the current iteration while forgetting past experiences, e.g. state-action pairs encountered only a few iterations ago.
elements.sent-2411-201: To address this, we enlarge the language value function training set by merging the last $K_{buffer}$ value function buffers, which help stabilize the training of the language value function by preserving past knowledge.

elements.sent-2411-248: Successor features \citep{dayan1993improving, barreto2017successor aim to learn a high-dimensional value function representation, where each component captures the expected future occurrence of all state representations under a fixed policy.}
elements.sent-2411-249: As shown in \citep{barreto2017successor , successor features help decouple the environment dynamics from the rewards, facilitating transfer learning in RL.}

elements.sent-2411-251: In this work, we propose a new paradigm NLRL, which reformulates the RL process with natural language representation.
elements.sent-2411-252: We believe NLRL opens a new door for understanding and implementing RL algorithms in natural language space, improving agent's effectiveness, efficiency and interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-253: NLRL is also compatible with the current language model and LLM-based agent.

elements.sent-2411-255: Currently, NLRL still faces several limitations.
elements.sent-2411-256: First, our experiments demonstrate its effectiveness primarily in environments with discrete action spaces and low-dimensional (textualisable) states.
elements.sent-2411-257: Its performance in environments involving continuous action spaces and high-dimensional states (e.g., low-level robotic control tasks) remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-258: Second, our experiments are primarily conducted in small-scale settings due to the substantial computational cost associated with invoking large language models.
elements.sent-2411-259: The time efficiency of the current NLRL approach is significantly lower than that of traditional RL methods using smaller networks.
elements.sent-2411-260: Given NLRL's potential, there are several promising directions for future research.
elements.sent-2411-261: First, a theoretical foundation for the entire framework in the language space is needed to move beyond analogical reasoning towards a more robust, principled basis.
elements.sent-2411-262: This would help formalise the framework and enhance its generalisability.
elements.sent-2411-263: Another important direction is exploring how NLRL can be integrated more organically with existing research on self-evaluation, self-improvement, LLM agent framework, and LLM's planning.
elements.sent-2411-264: Such integration could unlock new capabilities and efficiency improvements.
elements.sent-2411-265: Additionally, in terms of application domains, there is significant potential to extend NLRL beyond the current setups--we are actively exploring extending NLRL to general domains beyond games, such as reasoning tasks \citep{cobbe2021gsm8k and code \citep{chen2021evaluating .}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Language GPI by Prompting} \label{algo:language_gpi}\begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Initial language policy $\pi$, language value function $V_\pi^L$, LLM implementing operators $G_1$, $G_2$, policy improvement operator $I$, number of lookahead steps $N$, number of variations $K$ \FOR{each state $s_t$} \STATE // Language TD estimation \FOR{$i=1$ to $K$} \STATE Sample action $a_t^{(i)}$ (\ding{172}) \STATE Simulate $N$ steps ahead to get trajectory $\tau^{(i)}$ \STATE Prompt LLM to evaluate $V_\pi^L(s_{t+N}^{(i)})$ (\ding{173}) \STATE Generate intermediate description $d^{(i)} = d(a_t^{(i)}, r_t^{(i)}, s_{t+1}^{(i)})$ \STATE Compute value estimate $V^{(i)} = G_2(d^{(i)}, V_\pi^L(s_{t+N}^{(i)}))$ (\ding{174}) \ENDFOR \STATE Aggregate value estimates $V_\pi^L(s_t) = G_1(\{V^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{K})$ (\ding{174}) \STATE // Policy improvement \STATE Use $V_{\pi}^L(s_t)$ and prompt LLM as operator $I$ to select the optimal action $a_t$ (\ding{176}) \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.subsection-2411-011: Train natural language language value function with language TD
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Train natural language language value function with language TD} \label{alg:nl_language_td} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Pre-defined policy $\pi$, LLM-based language value model $V^L_\pi$, language descriptor $d$, language aggregator $G_1$, $G_2$, state buffer $D_s$, number of iterations $T$, number of trajectories $N$, number of MC trajectories $K$ \STATE // Collect few-step rollout data using fixed policy for TD data buffer \STATE Initialize TD data buffer $\mathcal{B} \leftarrow \{\}$ \FOR{state $s_t$ in state buffer $D_s$} \STATE Starting from $s_t$, run policy $\pi$ for a few $l$-step look-ahead rollouts and collect $K$ distinct variations: $V_{s_t}=\{(s_{t+i}, a_{t+i}, r_{t+i}, s_{t+i+1})_{i=0}^{l-1}\}_{K}$ \STATE $\mathcal{B} \leftarrow \mathcal{B} \cup V_{s_t}$ \ENDFOR \STATE // Language TD learning \FOR{iteration $t=1$ to $T$} \STATE // Language TD estimate \ding{173}, \ding{174} \STATE $\mathcal{D}_V \leftarrow \{\}$ \COMMENT{Value training data} \FOR{each look-ahead data $(s_t, V_{s_t})$ in $\mathcal{B}$} \STATE For each variation $n$, generate the final state evaluation $V_{\pi}^L(s_{t+l}^{n})$ with $V_{\pi}^L$, and utilize $d$ to describe intermediate transitions $d_n=d(s_{t}, a_{t}^{n}, r_{t}^{n}, s_{t+1}^{n},..., s_{t+l-1}^{n}, a_{t+l-1}^{n}, r_{t+l-1}^{n})$ \STATE $V_\pi^{L}(s_t)_{new} \leftarrow G_1\Big(\big\{G_2\big(d_n,V_\pi^L(s_{t+1}^n)\big)\big\}_{n=1}^K \Big)$ \COMMENT{l-step, k-variation Language TD} \STATE $\mathcal{D}_V \leftarrow \mathcal{D}_V \cup V_\pi^{L}(s_t)_{new}$ \ENDFOR \STATE // Update value function \ding{175} \STATE Train $V^L_\pi$ using language TD estimate dataset $\mathcal{D}_V$ with supervised loss \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Natural Language Actor-Critic Learning} \label{alg:nl_actor_critic} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Initial language policy model $\pi_L$, language value model $V^L_\pi$, large language model for evaluation $G_1$/$G_1$,$G_2$, improvement operator $I$ \REQUIRE Number of iterations $T$, number of trajectories $N$, number of MC trajectories $K$ \STATE Initialize replay buffer history $\mathcal{B} \leftarrow \{\}$ \FOR{iteration $t=1$ to $T$} \STATE // Collect trajectories using language policy \ding{172} \STATE $\tau \leftarrow \{\}$ elements.sent-2411-303: For the policy $\pi$, as mentioned in the paper, we choose an MCTS policy with $uct_c=1$, 1000 simulations for search, and 100 rollouts for the random rollout evaluator.

elements.sent-2411-309: To generate the mixed state dataset, we pair every possible combination of these 16 policies (including self-pairing), leading to \(16 \times 16 = 256\) policy pairs.

elements.sent-2411-312: By merging the data from all policy pairs, we create the final mixed state dataset \(D_s\), which is designed to represent states arising from a wide range of gameplay strategies and skill levels.

elements.sent-2411-352: Our experiments are conducted on the Tic-tac-toe environment~\citep{gym_tictactoe , a standard two-player game environment that provides a clear testbed for evaluating sequential decision-making.}
elements.sent-2411-353: The implementation uses several key components: \url{https://github.com/haje01/gym-tictactoe for the Tic-tac-toe environment, FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) for distributed model training, vLLM~\citep{kwon2023efficient for efficient language model inference, and a custom parallel framework for trajectory collection.}}
elements.sent-2411-354: The environment is wrapped with a parallel execution layer to enable efficient batch processing of multiple games simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-355: For policy evaluation, we employ Monte Carlo sampling to estimate state-action values, requiring complete game trajectories until terminal states.
elements.sent-2411-356: Policy improvement utilizes a structured sampling approach with temperature-controlled exploration and frequency-based action selection.
elements.sent-2411-357: To manage computational resources efficiently, we employ gradient checkpointing and use the FSDP strategy across our GPU cluster.
elements.sent-2411-358: This configuration allows us to train larger language models while maintaining reasonable memory requirements and training speed.
elements.sent-2411-359: The parallel data collection framework is designed to maximize GPU utilization during training.
elements.sent-2411-360: It maintains a queue of active environments and processes state-action transitions in batches, significantly reducing the overall training time compared to sequential execution.

elements.sent-2411-421: For our PPO baseline, we implement a custom PPO agent adapted for language models.
elements.sent-2411-422: The agent consists of two key components: an actor network based on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct for action selection, and a critic network for value estimation.
elements.sent-2411-423: The actor network uses LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct's architecture with added special tokens for moves 1-9.
elements.sent-2411-424: The critic network consists of a two-layer MLP (Multi-Layer Perceptron) that takes the last hidden state from the actor as input:.

elements.sent-2411-611: Artificial intelligence progresses towards the "Era of Experience," where agents are expected to learn from continuous, grounded interaction.
elements.sent-2411-612: We argue that traditional Reinforcement Learning (RL), which typically represents value as a scalar, can restrict agent's deep understanding of environments and hinders the active, deliberative learning crucial for navigating this new paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-613: To address the issue, we introduce Natural Language Reinforcement Learning (NLRL), a framework that extends RL principles into natural language counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-614: Central to NLRL is the Language Value Function (LVF), which redefines value as an interpretable linguistic narrative articulating the rationale behind an evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-615: NLRL further extends this concept to core RL components, including policy, the Bellman equation, and policy iteration.
elements.sent-2411-616: Leveraging recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), NLRL can be practically implemented to achieve RL-like policy and value training through unsupervised environment interactions.
elements.sent-2411-617: Experiments over 4 multi-step agentic tasks demonstrate NLRL's effectiveness, efficiency, and its potential to foster deeper understanding and more active learning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-618: The pursuit of artificial intelligence is rapidly advancing towards the "Era of Experience," where agents are expected to learn and master complex tasks through continuous, grounded interaction with their environments, thereby moving beyond the inherent limitations of pre-existing human data \citep{silver2025welcome .}
elements.sent-2411-619: Central to this paradigm shift is the ability of agents to not only act but also to deeply understand and evaluate their experiences to inform future decisions.
elements.sent-2411-620: Traditional Reinforcement Learning (RL) \citep{sutton2018reinforcement has laid a robust mathematical foundation for such decision-making, primarily through the concept of a value function, which estimates the cumulative reward of states or state-action pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-621: The conventional representation of value is scalar quantity \citep{sutton1999policy , acting as highly compressed summaries of future trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-622: While computationally convenient for optimization, it fundamentally limits an agent's capacity for a deep, structural understanding of its experiences.
elements.sent-2411-623: This limitation becomes particularly salient when considering the vast, nuanced information embedded within an agent's interaction stream – information that a single numerical value struggles to encapsulate.
elements.sent-2411-624: For instance, why is a particular state valuable? Is it due to an immediate tactical advantage, a long-term strategic opportunity, a successful mitigation of a threat, or a combination thereof? A scalar value provides the "what" (how good) but not the "why." Without this crucial "why," the agent operates with a superficial grasp of its environment and the true drivers of long-term success.
elements.sent-2411-625: This superficial understanding, in turn, shapes the very nature of the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-626: While policy optimization methods like policy gradients \citep{williams1992simple, sutton1999policy effectively reinforce successful behaviors, their efficacy is largely predicated on a "passive" discovery through sampling: the agent can only refine its strategy by first observing the outcomes of sampled actions, essentially waiting to stumble upon and then amplify beneficial action sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-627: Without a richer, more interpretable grasp of why certain actions yield better outcomes, it is challenging to shift from passive sampling.
elements.sent-2411-628: It cannot engage in "active" or "deliberative" reasoning over the root causes of success or failure to proactively and systematically adjust its strategies.
elements.sent-2411-629: To truly unlock the potential of experiential learning and build agents capable of nuanced understanding, transparent reasoning, and more active, deliberative learning, we argue for a richer, more expressive representation of value function.
elements.sent-2411-630: In this paper, we propose a novel approach, Natural Language Reinforcement Learning (NLRL), that directly addresses this challenge by re-envisioning the core components of RL within the domain of natural language.
elements.sent-2411-631: At the heart of NLRL is the concept of the Language Value Function (LVF).
elements.sent-2411-632: Instead of a simple scalar, the LVF redefines value as a rich, interpretable linguistic narrative.
elements.sent-2411-633: LVF allows an agent to articulate why a state or action is valuable, drawing justifications from textualized experiences and observations.
elements.sent-2411-634: This richer representation of value is crucial for learning actively from the diverse and often nuanced feedback characteristic of the "Era of Experience." Building upon the Language Value Function, our NLRL framework further extends other key RL elements – including policy, Bellman equation, and policy iteration – into their language counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-635: We demonstrate how these components can be practically implemented with the recent success of large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-636: Our experiments in 4 multi-step agentic environments showcase the effectiveness, efficiency, and particularly potentials for more active learning within the NLRL framework compared to traditional RL approaches, paving the way for agents that learn not just to act, but to understand, reason, and actively improve.

elements.sent-2411-658: To overcome these limitations, we argue for a different approach to compressing future rollouts.
elements.sent-2411-659: Instead of collapsing the future into a single number, we propose Language Value Function, that leverages the expressive power of natural language, processed by the underlying LLM, to create a richer, more informative summary.
elements.sent-2411-660: This approach offers several potential advantages.
elements.sent-2411-661: First, it utilizes the LLM's inherent language understanding, reasoning and summarization capabilities, potentially drawing upon its pre-trained knowledge to generate high-quality, semantically meaningful descriptions of future possibilities.
elements.sent-2411-662: Second, it retains significantly more information than a scalar value – capturing aspects of the strategy, reasoning steps, potential pitfalls, or key events – without the impracticality of storing or processing entire raw trajectory distributions.
elements.sent-2411-663: This strikes a balance, achieving useful compression without excessive information loss.

$V^L_\pi$, large language model for evaluation $G_1$/$G_1$,$G_2$, improvement operator $I$ \REQUIRE Number of iterations $T$, number of trajectories $N$, number of MC trajectories $K$ \STATE Initialize replay buffer history $\mathcal{B} \leftarrow \{\}$ \FOR{iteration $t=1$ to $T$} \STATE // Collect trajectories using language policy \ding{172} \STATE $\tau \leftarrow \{\}$ \COMMENT{Initialize trajectory buffer} \FOR{$i=1$ to $N$} \STATE Run policy $\pi_L$ to collect trajectory: $\tau_i \leftarrow \{(s_j, a_j, r_j, s_{j+1})_{j=0}^{H_i}\}$ \STATE $\tau \leftarrow \tau \cup \{\tau_i\}$ \ENDFOR \STATE // Language value estimation \ding{173}, \ding{174} \STATE $\mathcal{D}_V \leftarrow \{\}$ \COMMENT{Value training data} \FOR{each state-action pair $(s, a)$ in $\tau$} \IF{using MC estimate} \STATE Sample $K_{MC}$ trajectories starting from $(s, a)$: $\{\tau_k\}_{k=1}^{K_{MC}}$ \STATE $v \leftarrow G_1(\{\tau_k\}_{k=1}^{K_{MC}})$ \COMMENT{Language MC estimate} \ELSIF{using TD estimate} \STATE Sample $K_{TD}$ transitions starting from $(s, a)$: $\{(s_k, a_k, r_k, s'_k)\}_{k=1}^{K_{TD}}$ \STATE $v \leftarrow G_1(\{G_2(d(s_k,a_k,r_k,s'_k), V^L_\pi(s'_k))\}_{k=1}^{K_{TD}})$ \COMMENT{Language TD estimate} \ENDIF \STATE $\mathcal{D}_V \leftarrow \mathcal{D}_V \cup \{(s, a, v)\}$ \ENDFOR \STATE // Update value function \ding{175} \STATE $\mathcal{B} \leftarrow \mathcal{B} \cup \{\mathcal{D}_V\}$ \COMMENT{Add to buffer history} \STATE Train $V^L_\pi$ on merged data from last $K_{buffer}$ buffers in $\mathcal{B}$ with supervised loss \STATE // Language policy improvement \ding{176} \STATE $\mathcal{D}_\pi \leftarrow \{\}$ \COMMENT{Policy training data} \FOR{each state $s$ in $\tau$} \STATE Sample actions $\{a_i\}_{i=1}^{N_{sample}}$ from $\pi_L(s)$ \STATE Select top-$m$ actions $\mathcal{A}_k$ based on sampling frequency \STATE Query value estimates: $Q^L \leftarrow \{V^L_\pi(s,a) | a \in \mathcal{A}_k\}$ \STATE $\pi_{new}, c \leftarrow I(Q^L)$ \COMMENT{Language improvement operator} \STATE $\mathcal{D}_\pi \leftarrow \mathcal{D}_\pi \cup \{(s, \pi_{new}, c)\}$ \ENDFOR \STATE // Update policy \ding{177} \STATE Train $\pi_L$ on $\mathcal{D}_\pi$ with supervised loss \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-819: Due to high computational burdens, the paper does not include error bars for all of our experiments, but the paper contains every detail necessary for experiment reproduction.. \item[] Guidelines:.
elements.sent-2411-820: The authors should answer "Yes" if the results are accompanied by error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests, at least for the experiments that support the main claims of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-821: The factors of variability that the error bars are capturing should be clearly stated (for example, train/test split, initialization, random drawing of some parameter, or overall run with given experimental conditions).
elements.sent-2411-822: The method for calculating the error bars should be explained (closed form formula, call to a library function, bootstrap, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-823: The assumptions made should be given (e.g., Normally distributed errors).
elements.sent-2411-824: It should be clear whether the error bar is the standard deviation or the standard error of the mean.
elements.sent-2411-825: It is OK to report 1-sigma error bars, but one should state it.
elements.sent-2411-826: The authors should preferably report a 2-sigma error bar than state that they have a 96\% CI, if the hypothesis of Normality of errors is not verified.
elements.sent-2411-827: For asymmetric distributions, the authors should be careful not to show in tables or figures symmetric error bars that would yield results that are out of range (e.g. negative error rates).
elements.sent-2411-828: If error bars are reported in tables or plots, The authors should explain in the text how they were calculated and reference the corresponding figures or tables in the text.
elements.sent-2411-829: The paper should indicate the type of compute workers CPU or GPU, internal cluster, or cloud provider, including relevant memory and storage.
elements.sent-2411-830: The paper should provide the amount of compute required for each of the individual experimental runs as well as estimate the total compute.
elements.sent-2411-831: The paper should disclose whether the full research project required more compute than the experiments reported in the paper (e.g., preliminary or failed experiments that didn't make it into the paper).

elements.sent-2411-837: Examples of negative societal impacts include potential malicious or unintended uses (e.g., disinformation, generating fake profiles, surveillance), fairness considerations (e.g., deployment of technologies that could make decisions that unfairly impact specific groups), privacy considerations, and security considerations.
elements.sent-2411-838: The conference expects that many papers will be foundational research and not tied to particular applications, let alone deployments.
elements.sent-2411-839: However, if there is a direct path to any negative applications, the authors should point it out.
elements.sent-2411-840: For example, it is legitimate to point out that an improvement in the quality of generative models could be used to generate deepfakes for disinformation.
elements.sent-2411-841: On the other hand, it is not needed to point out that a generic algorithm for optimizing neural networks could enable people to train models that generate Deepfakes faster.
elements.sent-2411-842: The authors should consider possible harms that could arise when the technology is being used as intended and functioning correctly, harms that could arise when the technology is being used as intended but gives incorrect results, and harms following from (intentional or unintentional) misuse of the technology.
elements.sent-2411-843: If there are negative societal impacts, the authors could also discuss possible mitigation strategies (e.g., gated release of models, providing defenses in addition to attacks, mechanisms for monitoring misuse, mechanisms to monitor how a system learns from feedback over time, improving the efficiency and accessibility of ML).
